description: All vehicles entering Olympic National Park must pay an entrance fee or hold an America the Beautiful pass.
where: Pay at entrance stations or purchase online
cost: $30 per vehicle (7-day pass) · $55 annual Olympic pass · $80 America the Beautiful annual pass

seasonalWindow.notes: Fee applies year-round at staffed entrance stations. Some remote access points are self-pay.

tips: The America the Beautiful pass ($80/year) covers all national parks and is worth it if you visit two or more parks.
The Olympic-specific annual pass ($55) is good value for repeat visitors to the peninsula.
Fee-free days occur several times a year — check the NPS calendar.

description: All overnight stays in the Olympic backcountry require a wilderness camping permit. Day hiking does not require a permit. Some popular areas (High Divide, Enchanted Valley, Grand Valley) require advance reservations during peak season.
where: recreation.gov (advance) or Wilderness Information Center in Port Angeles (walk-up)
cost: $8 per person per night (reservation fee) + $6 per reservation (online booking fee)

seasonalWindow.notes: Advance reservations for peak-season permits open in early spring. Walk-up permits available at the Wilderness Information Center, first-come-first-served.

tips: High Divide / Seven Lakes Basin is the most competitive permit — apply early in the reservation window.
Walk-up permits are available for less popular areas and shoulder season dates.
Bear canisters are required in some areas — check regulations for your route.
Group size limited to 12 in wilderness areas.

description: Kalaloch Campground accepts reservations via recreation.gov. Hoh, Sol Duc, and other campgrounds are first-come-first-served or reservable depending on season.

seasonalWindow.notes: Kalaloch and Sol Duc campgrounds are reservable in peak season. Hoh Campground is first-come-first-served year-round.

tips: Kalaloch bluff sites are the most coveted — book 6 months out for summer weekends.
Hoh Campground rarely fills on weekdays; arrive by noon on summer weekends.
Sol Duc Campground's proximity to the hot springs makes it a popular choice — book ahead.
Winter camping is available at Kalaloch and Hoh year-round.

description: A Makah Recreation Pass ($10/vehicle) is required to access Cape Flattery, Shi Shi Beach, and other Makah Reservation lands. Supports Makah cultural preservation and trail maintenance.
where: Makah Museum, Neah Bay businesses, or makah.com

seasonalWindow.notes: Purchase before arriving at the trailhead. Available at the Makah Museum, the Makah Marina, and several Neah Bay businesses.

tips: The permit is separate from the National Park entrance fee.
Combine with a visit to the Makah Museum — one of the most significant Indigenous cultural institutions in the Pacific Northwest.
Cape Flattery is the northwesternmost point in the contiguous United States.
The trail is 1.5 miles round trip and moderately rooted — wear sturdy shoes.
